text: The rapid maturation of deep learning has catalyzed the development of sophisticated generative models capable of addressing the inherent limitations of real-world time series datasets. Deep neural networks are notoriously data-hungry, yet time series data in practical applications—ranging from finance to healthcare—are often characterized by scarcity, class imbalance, and strict privacy constraints. To mitigate these challenges, data augmentation (DA) has emerged as a vital strategy for expanding training sets and enhancing model robustness. This paper provides a comprehensive survey and a novel taxonomy of the current state of the art in time series data augmentation. We categorize methodologies into traditional transformation-based techniques, such as jittering, scaling, and permutation, and advanced generative frameworks, including Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). Furthermore, we review specialized alignment-based methods like Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) barycenter averaging. The survey critically evaluates the specific strengths and weaknesses of each approach, discusses the complexities of performance metrics and evaluation strategies, and identifies persistent challenges such as training instability and the preservation of temporal dependencies. By synthesizing the evolution of the field and categorizing the most effective algorithms, this study serves as a foundational guide for researchers seeking to optimize lead-time and accuracy in time series modeling through synthetic data enrichment.
